worth_it_explanation: Block and allow list controls in DV Pinnacle determine which inventory a campaign can reach before the auction, not after. A blocklist entry that is too broad suppresses valid inventory without appearing in standard campaign reporting; a blocklist that has never been updated leaves newly problematic inventory unblocked. Neither outcome shows up as a brand safety event, so the gap can persist for months without a deliberate review. These checks are read-only inspections inside DV Pinnacle and require no external tooling. The practitioner reads the current lists, compares them against documented brand policy, and records whether the controls reflect intentional decisions rather than defaults carried forward from account launch.
intro: DoubleVerify (DV) Pinnacle gives advertisers manual block and allow controls that operate at the inventory level, separate from DV's automated content classification. Three mechanisms make up the control layer: domain and app blocklists, domain and app allowlists, and keyword avoidance lists applied to page uniform resource locators (URLs). These checks confirm that each mechanism is actively configured, reflects current brand policy, and is not suppressing valid inventory through entries that have become stale or overreaching since the account was set up.

audit_checks.title: High-sensitivity campaigns rely on blocklists alone, with no allowlist to protect reach
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A blocklist is an exclusion of known-bad inventory. It cannot account for inventory that has not yet been reviewed or categorized. For campaigns where brand proximity carries significant risk, a blocklist-only posture means the campaign serves on any inventory DV has not yet flagged, including inventory that would fail a brand review if it had been classified. An allowlist inverts the logic: the campaign serves only on inventory the brand has explicitly approved, regardless of whether the rest of the pool has been classified.

educate.base:
DoubleVerify Pinnacle provides two complementary list-based controls: blocklists and allowlists. They operate differently and suit different risk profiles.

A domain or app blocklist tells DV's pre-bid filter to exclude specific domains or applications from the campaign's eligible inventory pool. The campaign can serve anywhere else DV's classification passes. This approach scales well for campaigns where most open-web inventory is acceptable: the list removes a defined set of problem placements and the campaign runs freely across the remainder. The weakness is coverage. DV's classification reviews inventory continuously, but the universe of unclassified inventory is large. A blocklist cannot exclude what has not yet been reviewed.

A domain or app allowlist restricts the campaign to a pre-approved set of placements. The campaign cannot serve on any domain or app outside the list, whether or not DV has reviewed it. This approach is more restrictive by design and requires the list to be built thoughtfully so it does not eliminate placements the brand wants to reach. The benefit is certainty: every impression runs in an environment the brand has explicitly approved.

A few signs point to a blocklist-only posture on campaigns where an allowlist would serve the brand better:
  • The campaign's placement settings in DV Pinnacle show a blocklist applied but no allowlist configured
  • The campaign targets sensitive audience segments (e.g., children, healthcare, financial products) or carries messaging where proximity to unreviewed content carries meaningful risk
  • The brand's documented brand safety policy specifies that inventory must be pre-approved rather than assumed acceptable by default
You may want to confirm whether the campaign's risk profile warrants an allowlist before treating a blocklist as sufficient. The MRC Ad Verification Supplement for Brand Safety describes minimum baseline controls that MRC-accredited verification vendors are expected to support, which can help frame what level of control is appropriate for a given campaign context.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Retailers selling products to children, products regulated by age, or products in categories with heightened proximity sensitivity (e.g., alcohol, firearms) may want to confirm that campaigns carrying those products use allowlist controls rather than blocklist-only. Blocklist coverage gaps in unclassified inventory are a particular concern for regulated-product campaigns where a single adjacency incident can attract regulatory attention.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, a blocklist-only posture may be appropriate for broad awareness campaigns but insufficient for campaigns targeting financial services, healthcare, or legal audiences where the lead quality and brand reputation are tightly linked. You may want to confirm whether the risk profile of each campaign's audience justifies an allowlist for that specific flight.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Ad Verification Supplement: Enhanced Content Level Context and Brand Safety

educate.learn_more_note: DoubleVerify's documentation for configuring domain and app blocklists and allowlists in DV Pinnacle is available within the platform's help center, which requires a DV Pinnacle login to access. Your DV account team or implementation specialist can walk through the specific configuration steps for your account.

investigate.intro: Open DV Pinnacle and review the placement-level controls for the campaigns you are auditing, focusing on whether blocklists and allowlists are each present or absent for campaigns that carry elevated brand risk.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the advertiser or campaign you are auditing.
Open the brand safety or placement controls section. Look for separate entries for domain/app blocklists and domain/app allowlists.
Note whether an allowlist is configured. If no allowlist exists, record that the campaign relies on blocklist exclusions and DV's automated classification to determine eligible inventory.

Identify any campaigns that target audiences or carry messaging in categories where unreviewed inventory poses a meaningful adjacency risk (e.g., campaigns targeting minors, financial services campaigns, healthcare campaigns). For each such campaign, note whether it operates with a blocklist-only posture.

If an allowlist exists, review a sample of the entries to confirm the list reflects current approved placements rather than a default or inherited list that has not been reviewed since the account launched.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether each audited campaign has an allowlist configured in addition to any blocklist. Campaigns operating with a blocklist-only posture pass when the campaign's risk profile is low and the brand's documented policy does not require pre-approved inventory. Campaigns operating with a blocklist-only posture fail when the campaign targets sensitive audiences, carries messaging in regulated categories, or when the brand's policy specifies that inventory must be explicitly approved. The fail is recorded with the campaign name, the absence of an allowlist, and the specific risk factor that makes the blocklist-only posture insufficient.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the placement control configuration for each audited campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s high-sensitivity campaigns in DoubleVerify Pinnacle rely on domain and app blocklists without a corresponding allowlist. A blocklist excludes known-problematic inventory but cannot account for inventory that has not yet been reviewed or classified by DV. For campaigns where brand proximity carries meaningful risk, this posture means the campaign serves on unreviewed inventory by default. {{voice_recommend}} evaluating whether each campaign's risk profile warrants an allowlist configuration that restricts delivery to explicitly approved placements, rather than treating all unblocked inventory as acceptable.

audit_checks.title: Domain and app blocklists are stale, overbroad, or have never been reviewed since account launch
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A blocklist that has not been reviewed since account setup creates two distinct risks that pull in opposite directions. Entries added when the account launched may no longer reflect current policy or current domain behavior, blocking inventory the brand would now accept and suppressing reach without a corresponding safety benefit. At the same time, a list that was never updated cannot account for domains or apps that have become problematic since launch. Both outcomes go undetected without a deliberate review cycle.

educate.base:
A domain or app blocklist in DoubleVerify Pinnacle is a manually maintained list. DV's automated classification runs continuously and updates the pool of inventory available to a campaign, but the blocklist itself reflects whatever entries were added by the account team at setup or during a past review cycle. The list does not self-update.

Two failure modes follow from this. First, overblocking: entries added for a domain that no longer carries problematic content, or entries added for a broad domain pattern that blocks an entire publisher network rather than a specific section, suppress inventory the campaign could safely reach. This is a reach and efficiency problem. Second, underblocking: domains or applications that developed brand safety concerns after the list was last reviewed are not blocked. No one has added them. The campaign serves on them as though they passed review.

In March 2025, DoubleVerify announced a policy change to restrict keyword blocking to unclassified URLs only, citing evidence that stale keyword lists were blocking inventory on classified publisher pages that had already passed DV's content review. The same maintenance problem applies to domain and app blocklists: entries that once had a clear rationale become overbroad as the domain's content changes or as DV's classification catches up to the inventory pool.

A few signs point to a stale or overbroad blocklist:
  • The blocklist contains entries that predate any brand safety review by more than 12 months and have not been audited since
  • The blocklist contains wildcard or broad-match domain patterns that block entire publisher groups rather than specific problem placements
  • The blocklist contains entries for domains that DV has since classified as passing brand safety checks, meaning the manual block duplicates DV's automated exclusion and the rationale for the manual entry may no longer hold
You may want to request a blocklist export from DV Pinnacle and review the entry dates and any notes attached to each entry before deciding which to retain.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Retailers that ran brand safety reviews during a period of heightened news sensitivity (e.g., a product recall cycle, a social media controversy, a seasonal brand protection push) may have added blocklist entries that reflect that moment rather than an ongoing policy. You may want to confirm that entries added during such periods still reflect current brand risk rather than a moment-specific reaction.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead generation advertisers that block domains based on historical conversion quality data should confirm that those entries still reflect current domain performance rather than performance from a prior campaign flight. Domains that produced low-quality leads in one context may perform differently in a new campaign structure or with a new audience definition.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Ad Verification Supplement: Enhanced Content Level Context and Brand Safety

educate.learn_more_note: DV Pinnacle's interface for exporting and reviewing blocklist entries, including any metadata about when entries were added, is accessible only with a DV Pinnacle login. Your DV account team can generate a blocklist export for an offline review.

impactful_updates.update_title: DV restricts keyword blocking to unclassified URLs only (March 2025)
impactful_updates.update_description: DoubleVerify announced in March 2025 that keyword avoidance would apply only to URLs that DV has not yet classified into its content category taxonomy. Pages already classified by DV are excluded from keyword blocking, on the grounds that classified inventory has passed DV's content review and blanket keyword blocks were suppressing it unnecessarily. The same maintenance logic applies to domain and app blocklists: stale entries on classified inventory reduce reach without a corresponding safety benefit.

investigate.intro: Export or review the domain and app blocklists in DV Pinnacle and assess whether the entries reflect a current, reviewed policy or inherited defaults that have not been audited.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the brand safety or placement controls section for the advertiser you are auditing.
Open the domain and app blocklist. Note the total number of entries and, if the interface displays it, when the list was last modified or when individual entries were added.

Review a sample of entries, focusing on any that appear to be broad domain patterns (e.g., a top-level domain that would block an entire publisher rather than a specific section). Note any entries where the rationale for blocking is not documented.

Check whether any entries duplicate coverage already provided by DV's automated content classification. If DV has classified a domain as passing brand safety, a manual blocklist entry for the same domain may be redundant and can be suppressing reach without a safety benefit.

Ask the account team or client when the list was last reviewed against the brand's current safety policy. If the answer is 'at account launch' or is unknown, record that the list has not been audited since setup.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the total number of blocklist entries, the date of the last documented review, and the presence of any broad-match or wildcard patterns. A blocklist that has been reviewed within the past 12 months against current brand policy and does not contain undocumented broad-match entries passes. A blocklist that has not been reviewed since account launch, contains entries with no documented rationale, or contains broad-match patterns that suppress entire publisher networks without a recorded review passes only if the account team can produce a current policy document that supports each pattern. Otherwise the blocklist is a fail, recorded with the specific entries that are candidates for removal or narrowing.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the blocklist review status and any entries flagged above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s domain and app blocklist in DoubleVerify Pinnacle has not been reviewed since account launch and contains entries that may no longer reflect current brand policy. Stale blocklist entries can suppress valid inventory and reduce campaign reach without a corresponding safety benefit, particularly as DV's automated classification progressively reviews the inventory pool. {{voice_recommend}} conducting a full blocklist review against the brand's current safety policy, removing entries that lack a documented rationale or that duplicate coverage DV's classification already provides, and establishing a review cadence of at least once per quarter to keep the list current.

audit_checks.title: Keyword avoidance lists are absent, stale, or have never been reviewed against current campaigns
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Keyword avoidance in DV Pinnacle operates on the URL of the page, not the full text of the content, and applies only to inventory that DV has not yet classified. This is a narrower control than it may appear: classified inventory is excluded from keyword blocking regardless of what the URL contains. An absent or stale keyword list leaves the brand with no URL-level adjacency control on unclassified inventory, which is the portion of the pool most likely to carry novel risk. The impact is moderate because DV's automated classification covers a large share of the inventory pool, but the gap is real for unclassified placements.

educate.base:
DoubleVerify's keyword avoidance control scans the URL of a page, treating the URL as a proxy for the page's subject matter. When a keyword in the advertiser's avoidance list matches a term that appears on its own in the URL string, DV's pre-bid filter excludes that impression. The control does not scan the body text of the page or the metadata beyond the URL.

As of March 2025, DV restricts keyword blocking to URLs that DV has not yet classified into its content taxonomy. Pages already classified by DV are excluded from keyword blocking because DV treats its classification as a more reliable signal than a URL-keyword match on already-reviewed inventory. This means keyword avoidance lists apply to the unclassified portion of the inventory pool, which is the segment where DV's automated review has not yet run.

A keyword avoidance list that was built at account launch and has not been updated since may fail in two directions. Terms added for a news cycle or campaign-specific context may now be blocking URL patterns that are no longer problematic, and terms that have become relevant since launch are not present. Because DV applies keyword blocking only to unclassified URLs, an outdated list does not cause overblocking of classified inventory, but it does leave the unclassified pool without current terms.

A few signs point to a keyword avoidance list that needs review:
  • The keyword avoidance list in DV Pinnacle is empty or contains only the DV-provided default terms with no advertiser-specific additions
  • The list has not been updated since the account was set up, and the brand's campaign messaging or category focus has changed since then
  • The list contains terms tied to a specific news event or product launch that concluded more than six months ago
You may want to review the current keyword list against the brand's documented off-limits terms before making changes. The IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy describes how content categories are defined at the industry level, which can help identify category-level controls that may cover ground the keyword list is trying to address through individual terms.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Retailers should confirm that keyword avoidance terms reflect the brand's current product lines and sensitivities rather than terms added for a past campaign or seasonal event. A keyword list built around a product recall or PR event may still be running long after the event has passed. You may also want to confirm that competitor brand names in the keyword list are intentional and still represent a blocking priority.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead generation advertisers often add keyword terms tied to specific audience concerns (e.g., terms related to financial distress, health emergencies, or legal trouble) to avoid adjacency that could undermine trust. You may want to confirm that the current terms still reflect the audiences each active campaign is targeting. A list built for one product line may not suit a different product's context.

educate.learn_more_note: DV Pinnacle's keyword avoidance configuration interface, including the ability to add, remove, and export keyword terms, requires a DV Pinnacle login. DV also offers an AI-powered keyword list optimization feature (announced March 2025) that identifies stale terms based on historical blocking data; access is through DV Pinnacle or your DV account team.

impactful_updates.update_title: DV restricts keyword blocking to unclassified URLs and introduces keyword list optimization (March 2025)
impactful_updates.update_description: DoubleVerify announced in March 2025 that keyword avoidance would apply only to URLs not yet classified by DV's content taxonomy system. DV also announced an AI-powered feature to help advertisers identify stale keyword terms based on historical blocking data, addressing the 'set it and forget it' problem where keyword lists suppress inventory that has already been reviewed and classified.

investigate.intro: Review the keyword avoidance list in DV Pinnacle and assess whether it reflects current brand policy and active campaign contexts.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the brand safety or keyword avoidance section for the advertiser you are auditing.
Open the keyword avoidance list. Note the total number of terms and whether the list is empty, contains only DV-provided default terms, or contains advertiser-specific entries.
If advertiser-specific terms are present, note when the list was last modified. If the interface does not show a modification date, ask the account team or client.

Review the terms against the brand's current campaign contexts. Identify any terms that were added for a past campaign or news event that is no longer active. Also note whether any terms relevant to the current campaign's content or audience context are absent from the list.
Note whether DV's AI-powered keyword optimization feature has been used to flag stale terms. If the feature has not been run, record that recommendation for the client.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the current state of the keyword avoidance list: whether it exists, how many terms it contains, when it was last reviewed, and whether any terms appear tied to past events or missing for current campaign contexts. A list that has been reviewed within the past six months against the brand's current campaign structure and documented policy passes. An empty list, a list containing only DV defaults with no advertiser-specific entries, or a list that has not been reviewed since account launch is a fail, recorded with the specific gap and the campaigns affected by the absence of current keyword controls.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the keyword avoidance list status and any specific gaps noted above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s keyword avoidance list in DoubleVerify Pinnacle has not been reviewed since account launch and does not reflect the brand's current campaign contexts or documented off-limits terms. Because DV applies keyword blocking only to unclassified URLs, the list's primary protective function is on the portion of the inventory pool that DV's automated classification has not yet reviewed. An outdated list leaves that segment without current brand-specific controls. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the keyword avoidance list against the brand's current safety policy and active campaign contexts, removing terms that are no longer relevant, adding terms that reflect current risks, and running DV's keyword optimization tool to identify terms that are blocking more inventory than intended.
